content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I’m done being an art snob, what’re some of the best dumb fun movies yall have for me

**Score:** 91
**Body:** I’ve seen plenty of artsy films, the godfather, all of Ghibli’s stuff, classics. I have neglected to watch “stupid” movies though, and I see the flaw in that now. I haven’t even seen like Jurassic park. Anyone got action movie dumb recs

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Big Trouble in Little China
  - He said he wasn't an art snob. This movie is a masterpiece.
2. Half Baked

Saving Silverman

Super Troopers 

Dumb & Dumber

Good Burger
3. Raising Arizona although it is dumb art 😁 
also Crimewave, also Grabbers
  - Raising Arizona is the best Cohen Brothers movie, I will die on this hill.
4. Cocaine Bear is the best movie featuring a bear on cocaine that I have ever watched.
5. Popstar: Never Stop Never Stopping
6. Leslie Nielsen movies — Naked Gun
7. The 80s were the best decade for mindless / fun comedies

 Better Off Dead

One Crazy Summer

Slapshot (70s)

Animal House (70s)

Caddyshack

Back to School

Old School

Anchorman

Dumb & Dumber

Risky Business

Happy Gilmore

Billy Madison

Raising Arizona

Fast Times at Ridgemont High

Spaceballs

Big Trouble in Little China

They Live
  - I've never really thought of Raising Arizona as a dumb comedy but it's been my favorite comedy of all time since I first saw it back in 1987. Nic Cage's greatest performance.

## 2. ZOMBIE MOVIES Recommendations!

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Hi! As a zombie-movie lover, I’m having difficulty finding movies or series to watch because I’ve already seen most of them (or at least I think I have).

I need help! I need recommendations for zombie movies or series. Please help a man out. 🥹😭

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Poultrygeist: Night of the Chicken Dead
2. *Train to Busan* 👌


*Shaun of the Dead* 👍👍


*Dead Snow* (Norwegian) Great fun
  - I forgot Shaun of the Dead. Worth a viewing just for the unique take
    - In the same "series", The World's End isn't exactly zombies, but essentially the same effect.
3. Someone dragged me to the Dawn of the Dead remake in 2004 even though I promised them I'd hate it and I actually loved it. Sprouted a whole obsession for the genre until it was ultimately oversaturated.

28 Days Later is a close second. 28 Weeks is surprisingly decent (unlike every Resident Evil movie after 2) haven't seen 28 Years Later because no one in my house can stomach excessive gore for some reason
  - The remake introduced me to Richard Cheese and, in my opinion, is the only tolerable Snyder film.
4. All of us are dead,  Kingdom both great zombie shows
5. Rec, in spanish.
6. Peter Jackson's Braindead is a treat! You'll never look at lawnmowers the same way again...
7. Resident Evil

Zombie Land

## 3. Who else has "The Lobster" living rent-free in their head?

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Am I the only one who suggests this movie to just about every one who asks me for a movie suggestion? The brilliant, bizarre, and deadpan satire on modern dating and societal pressure is just so unforgettable. I keep thinking about the rules of the hotel and the loners. This opened me up to the whole plethora of Yorgos and A24 movies out there!! 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm the same OP. This movie deeply touched me. I totally understand why many others do not like the movie. It's weird. Yet, I never get tired of recommding it.

But to me, it showed beautifully how society's expectations and pressure affect individuals. Like it expects you to fit into a specific mold to be deemed "worthy" part of the collective.

This is something I've struggled with all my life. I'm a strong character and I never liked when people expected me to "just fit in" with the majority. You might call it forced conformity. I hate that, always will.

But like I said,  I understand when others don't like the movie. It's absurd in a way. My wife hated it for example when we watched it together. I think it's a great film
  - I hear you! The film does suggest that society always imposes a rigid structure that demands you sacrifice your true self to be accepted.
2. Liked the first half not the second
3. I wonder ho many people would ask you for another advice after watching The Lobster because of your recommendation.

Don’t get me wrong, but I feel that movie is not for everyone, to say the least.
4. Me too! 

What i really like is that once you’re past the initial shock of the bizarre world, the movie stays great. 

I love that the loners initially look like the obvious rebels, but then their whole identity is still shaped by the world they’re reacting against. They do the opposite which isn’t better, more like just another version of the same thing. I find that weirdly realistic.

I could go on on a bunch of stuff, that movie is one of my favs!
  - Same! There is no true escape or freedom. It's "weirdly realistic" because we see this happen all the time in real-world social and political movements. It’s like it's hard to escape the original mold completely.
5. I loved the dry deadpan humour.
6. Can't watch it. Dog. I turned it off after that scene.
7. I liked the lobster a lot! My friend went to see Bugonia and said it was good. I also recommended him to see Beau is Afraid
8. I think I’ve told my coworkers about the Lobster a half dozen times, but I’ve found that leading a horse to water doesn’t translate to drinking. They’re missing out. The movie is pure genius.

## 4. Movies where the protagonist is being blackmailed?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I'm looking for movies where the protagonist is blackmailed and the plot gets interesting as to what the protagonist does because of the pressure.

Any genre or language if fine? Please suggest me.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not a movie but an episode, but it is interesting indeed

Black Mirror S03E03: Shut Up and Dance
2. Phone Booth (2002)
3. The Woman in the Window (1944), Strangers on a Train (1951).
4. My knowledge is limited but Derailed (2005) is one such movie.
5. Not a movie.  But man, Ozark hits this theme so damn hard.  Awesome show.
6. Only kind of fits, but "Killing of a Sacred Deer" i think has that tension you're looking for.
7. 

Blackmail (1929)

Firewall (2006)

Notes on a scandal (2006)

The invisible guest (2016)

Nick of time (1995)

The secret in their eyes (2009)

Cache (2005)

The unknown woman (2006)

Cash on demand (1961)

Birthday Girl (2001)
8. The Big Lebowski
9. Ransom
10. I believe this fits your description and I absolutely loved the twists its offered.


*Knock Goes Away* starring Michael Keaton and James Marsden.

## 5. Comically weird & disturbing films

**Score:** 18
**Body:** The Japanese probably have the best examples of these types of films, ones that have weird and messed up content within the narrative of the film but visuals and feeling play like a comic book film

I’m thinking films like Ichi The Killer, Suicide Club 

An American example would be the recently released Bugonia or even Midsommar.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Try *Brazil*.
2. The House That Jack Built is full of horrible stuff, and also the funniest movie I’ve seen in quite a while.
3. Rubber, about a sentient psychopathic car tyre
  - It’s like Anton Chigur (no country for old men) but in tyre format
4. 'Man Bites Dog' (1992)
5. The Lighthouse.  
6. Tusk - directed by Kevin Smith might fit this bill.  It’s surreal and absurd body horror with an unnamed cameo by Johnny Depp playing a quebecois cop.  Strong imagery throughout and great monologues.  

Pontypool is another - starring Stephen mchattie playing a rural Ontario local radio dj when a zombie virus outbreak occurs (spread by language instead of the usual bodily fluids.  A great single set film with a limited cast - high on the drama and narrative.

Hoping you watch both and tell me if I hit the vibe you’re looking for!
7. Visitor Q
8. The Greasy Strangler
9. Gummo

Bernie (1996) French film, hilarious then takes a dark turn (there's another "Bernie" with Jack Black as a funeral director that fits your request too I guess)

Greener Grass

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What happened to this guy?

**Score:** 698

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Couple of pipe hitting ******’s went to work on him with a pair of pliers and a blowtorch
  - They got medieval on his ass
    - Zed is dead
      - Seems like the gimp got off easy.
  - When people don't give Pulp Fiction it's dues as one the tops, just remind yourself how damn good the dialog is. This was the first quote that came to mind.

Done lost all his LA privileges.
  - "You hear that, hillbilly boy?! I ain't troo wit choo by a dayum sight!"
2. Greatest movie villain of 1994 twice 

Pulp Fiction and The Mask
  - Plus redfoot in Usual Suspects a year later
3. Peter Greene. Still gets regular work just nothing big budget
4. One things for sure, he didn’t die quickly

## 2. The movie “Hot Fuzz” made fun of the fact that cop movies tend to overlook the paper work part of the job. What are some other aspects of other occupations that movies tend to overlook?

**Score:** 577

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's often said that war is long periods of boredom punctuated by moments of sheer terror.

I think Platoon does a good job showcasing the boredom. Master and Commander does a great job of this at sea. Most war movies show off the action: Starship Troopers, Top Gun, The Alamo. Some TV shows have the time to develop this sense of ennui, like Band of Brothers. It's harder to do this in a movie. So it's nice to see it develop.
2. Movies always portray programmers as typing furiously.

The job is actually 95% staring silently at your computer screen and 5% typing.
3. Pegg and Frost read a book about action movie cliches and then tried putting as many of them in the movie, my kings.
  - Have you ever fired your gun up in the air and gone “aahhhhh”?
    - No I have never fired my gun up in the air and gone "aahh"
    - There was a bit earlier on that you missed, when, uh, I distracted him with the cuddly monkey and then I said "playtime's over" and I hit him over the head with a peace lily.
4. How much time in the military is spent literally bored out of your mind doing absolutely nothing and waiting for something to happen or something to do.
  - Jarhead (2005) speaks to this I think.
5. Architects: it's a profession of about 3% creativity and 97% regulatory compliance and making sure doors open the right way. You essentially draw every single part of a building line by line by line by damned line making sure the poop pipes don't interfere with the fresh air ducts and all of it is waterproof and won't fall down.
6. Most lawyers hardly ever go to court and even when they do, its usually very boring.

## 3. Let's settle it. Which is the better film:

**Score:** 409
**Body:** So much debate on which is the better Gothic horror. My vote is *Nosferatu*, but GDT did a heck of a job with *Frankenstein*

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 2025 has clearly been a decent year for gothic horror 
  - Nos came out in 2024
    - 2025 ± 1 year
      - The 20s!
        - The Horroring 20s?

The Goring 20s?

The Hor'ring 20s?

Maybe not the last one...
          - No no... maybe the last one...
2. I'll take the first half of Nosferatu and the 2nd half of Frankenstein lol
  - Making your own Frankenstein 
    - Nosferankenstein
      - Its the Monster Mash.

## 4. Which actor among them has the better filmography, both in terms of range and diversity of genres?

**Score:** 270
**Body:** 1st Pic - Leonardo DiCaprio Films

2nd Pic - Tom Cruise Films

3rd Pic - Christian Bale Films


### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Surely it’s Bale when it comes genre diversity? There are superhero movies, horror, comedy, sports, political, war etc.

I still prefer Leo’s filmography though out of these in terms of overall quality.
  - Yeah, honestly anyone saying Cruise has the best range hasnt seen enough films.


Action/thriller, or drama/thriller. Pretty much all his movies boil down to one or the other.


Edit: what he's done in the last 25 years can be put into these categories. 


Except Vanilla Sky. Mostly drama, little action.
    - Tropic Thunder is the best role he's ever played.
2. It bothers me that Leo's movies are shown in order, Bale's are ALMOST in order and Cruise's are random.
  - That bothered me, but also that only like 1/3 of Christian Bale’s films are even mentioned.
3. No newsies for Christian bale?
  - I  know! No Reign of Fire, The New World, 3:10 to Yuma, Hostiles, The Big Short, NO THE PALE BLUE EYE! And that’s just to name a few
4. Leo
5. Based on the criteria of your post, I'd have to choose Bale. But, in reviewing the films you've selected, I've had a few thoughts:

For some reason I always got the vibe that Leo was doing roles to hunt out an award. Good roles and equally good performances of course, but selected specifically because they could lead to an award consideration. 

Cruise seems like he just wants to entertain with occasional bits of wanting to flex his chops, but remains focused on just making a fun and entertaining experience. 

Bale seems like he's in it for the love of the game and never afraid to do something. But I generally get that vibe from most actors/actresses from the UK.
  - On that note about bale, I can’t wait to see him play Al Davis along side Nic cage as John madden.

## 5. ..What movie caused you to have a "WTF did I just watch?" moment after viewing?

**Score:** 216

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That Scarlett Johansson movie, where she's an alien or something, and doesn't talk. 

Gorgeous movie, but going in blind left me saying that before it even ended. 

Same with vivarium.
  - Under the Skin. Agreed came here to say that.
2. From this year... Bring Her Back.
  - Bugonia for me
3. Midsommer
  - Seriously. To add to that bizarness, at the part where they were tripping on the shrooms, we experienced an earthquake in our area. We could feel the seats shaking, but the thing was, myself and many other movie goesrs were confused as we could not tell if it was the effects of the movie or an actual earthquake giving us that feeling. Hard to explain unless you were there. 

Definitely left that theater in a daze due to that ending. With that said, I absolutely love that movie now.
4. Mother!
5. Lost highway
6. The substance
7. Blue fucking Velvet

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Comments by David Fincher on the director's commentary track for The Game (1997) that change how I see the film

**Score:** 180
**Body:** Maybe obvious to some but after listening to his comments I now feel like the movie had a lot more meta and deconstructive elements specifically about the artifice of filmmaking and the journey that they take an audience on, that audiences want to play "The Game", they just don't want to be reminded it's all fake.

Fincher on the commentary (not verbatim, kind of abridged from what he says over 2+ hours):

>For me this movie was an exercise in asking what does an audience WANT from a film, and what are they willing to put up with. Will they accept 45 minutes of red herrings? How far can you go before they reject it?

>

>"The Game" has a movie structure. Movie set pieces. The Big Con. These things don't happen in real life. People don't drug people in real life before robbing them, they just shoot them. They don't try to teach someone a lesson. It's only in a movie this happens where what matters is rubbing someone's nose in it, in big dramatic flourishes.

>Michael Douglas' character is trapped in a movie. That's what The Game offers, that's the personal adventure, and that's what people want. In the past, the personal adventure would be saying "do you want to travel around the world in a hot air balloon for 80 days?". But think about that, sitting in a balloon basket, freezing in the dark for 3 months where nothing much happens? People want something intricate and involving. That's what a 2 hour movie is. People paying to be trapped in this artificial creation. And they all stretch credibility and believability. So just how far can you stretch that before the audience snaps?

>

>You might think to yourself that it's impossible for the company in The Game to build a fake hospital that fools someone into believing it's real, and suddenly have the lights turn off and everyone is a fake doctor and it's all a con. But the very fact that we have this scene in the movie is proof that's possible. Like we actually DID that. The hospital in the movie is fake, it's a set, the doctors are actors. You believed it. And every scene in *every* movie is like that.

>

>At the end, entering the CRS building, is like the Mel Brooks Blazing Saddles moment where we're crashing through walls from set to set.

Made me think that really little of it is any more contrived or illogical than 99% of films, even "normal" ones like a basic rom-com which will be full of unlikely coincidence and probably resolve in some way designed to give a big emotional release, The Game is just showing you how it's done, almost like a Haneke approach where the thing that an audience hates the most and find the most intolerable is being "shown the puppet strings".

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This was my brothers favorite movie. I miss him, and think about it from time to time but makes it hard for me to rewatch.

This is, however, fascinating. Never quite thought about it that way. I think it’s time for a rewatch with this new perspective.
  - Your brother had good taste in movies. Really sorry for your loss.
2. That's interesting. There are parallels there with how I see The Prestige, in being about film itself (the audiences want/need for illusion, not wanting to see how the trick is done). Also I've seen a terrific analysis on Lost Highway being about a person trapped in a film and trying to break free. Who knew these three excellent films could belong in a nice meta trilogy. 
  - Not to mess up your unofficial trilogy, but Inception is famously a dead-on analogy for filmmaking. I also agree that The Prestige is a more interesting parallel to draw in this case.
3. I always felt similarly about Rear Window (that the subtext is about watching movies). Jeffries basically watches these lives outside his window like they're a movie but forgets these are real people, and ultimately the villain enters his domain and (SPOILER) tries to push him out through the movie screen (the window) into the movie.
  - At least with Rear Window it all means something personal. All the mini-movies Jeffries watches are possible scenes and phases from his and Grace Kelly's own lives if they get married or stay single. The subtext is whether he decides to marry her and whether the two of them will mesh with their different lifestyles. If they don't will he end up a frustrated artist, and will she end up a suicidal miss lonelyhearts? (She already indicates she relates to Miss Torso in having to fend off bland suitors.) If they marry will they be fun, "maladjusted misfits" like the couple who sleep on the fire escape together? Or will they end up nags wanting to kill each other, like Raymond Burr does? It's the ordeal a lot of people go through at the decision point of whether to choose a life together, and the empathy people experience watching (or over-watching, and judging) other people in those same situations.

The Game isn't quite as meaningful, in my opinion. He learns to let go of his rich-guy fixation on his father's depression and his own, but it seems almost like the movie treats that subtext as its own joke -- the propped-up excuse for the rest of the artifice.
4. This is my personal favorite of Fincher's films, and my favorite performance from Michael Douglas...his Wall Street type asshole that is a cover for sadness/depression and his sheer paranoia and the pitty you feel for him once he makes it back from Mexico.  Legit great acting.
5. I remember loving the movie at the theater and laughing off all the criticisms that were completely legitimate about how contrived it all was.  And that was because it was enormously entertaining and satisfying.  I never thought about any underlying meaning though and would love to revisit it now.  
6. Wow I  didn't realise that this was directed by Fincher - kinda wild it's in between Seven and Fight Club. It's been a long time since I've seen it, but I do remember thinking it was unbelievably stupid.

Looking back on it, I don't think the issues are with the audience being pushed to their limits of belief. If I'm remembering it right was it because Douglas is just a real passive character who can't influence the plot because he can't understand it himself. Like he's just running around having shit flung at him.

Totally dissimilar, but the Truman Show fundamentally doesn't make sense and could never happen in reality, but audiences can forgive that because  
\- they're in on the premise  
\- truman has real agency, and is the driving force behind the plot

The Game is the Truman show where Truman doesn't work anything out until the last scene when someone comes in and just tells him.

I think... please tell me if I'm wrong cause like I say it's been a while.

Definitely going to watch it again now I know its by Fincher and see if anything I'm saying holds.
  - I think your take is closest to my own. I don't think any movie that says, "Hey look! It's all fake!" is telling the audience anything they don't already know they're buying into, at some level. So the "revelation" of it is not impressive. 

I feel the same way about some of David Mamet's movies, like "The Spanish Prisoner." Oooh, there's a little con, but there's another bigger con -- and everything was fake from the beginning! Big whoop. The trick to doing this right is to have some larger point, beyond just "see how it's all fake? Aren't you AMAZED????"

## 2. "We Need To Talk About Kevin"; or, how a masterpiece gets overshadowed by its subject matter/theme

**Score:** 35
**Body:** *We Need To Talk About Kevin* (2011), directed by Lynne Ramsey and based on the great Lionel Shriver novel, is well-remembered for a nearly fifteen-year-old movie. I watched it soon after release and I recalled enjoying it the way one enjoys any particularly dreary or gruesome movie. I suspect it being "that movie about a school shooter" (especially in a culture that has further politicised the issues in year since), however, has distracted the public from a movie that is unbelievably well-made.

Ramsey and her co-writer take an unbelievably dense book and filter it through Ramsey's unique chopped-up visual and made it atmospheric to such a degree that that movie would be effective even if it had no dialogue or you watched it in a language you don't understand. I know a lot of people complained that the movie took away one of the book's central conceits (namely, was Kevin *really* evil from birth or Eva an unreliable narrator who created a school shooter because she never bonded with her son?). But a closer look at the movie's intense subjectivity reveals we are still entirely inside Eva's perspective; Ramsey turns the sound of an automatic sprinkler (something Eva recalls from the night of the incident) into the movie's most terrifying audio cue, all because we are so unbelievably inside her way of seeing things. The unreliable narrator aspect is not lost, people are just less likely to assume that a movie which depicts something happening on screen doesn't intend those scenes to be objective.

I could go on -- bottom line, Ramsey directed (regardless of its plot) a masterpiece. It sits, however, in the collective memory of the world as a mass shooter genre/horror-type film; her other movies, less distinctly classically violent in premise, are thought of as the art movies they are. There's nothing wrong with *Kevin* having been a 'simple' genre film; but it's not.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. To me there is too much horror of being misunderstood in the movie for it to be trying to maintain the ambiguity of whether Kevin is actually a psychopath or not. We see Eva desperately, repeatedly, trying to communicate what she is experiencing to other people, and we see Kevin intentionally changing his affect and lying to deceive these people and prevent them from believing her. The subjectivity is related to this dimension of the horror, since we the audience are right there with her and know that it is true, and we feel the world's unwillingness to listen to her and inability to see it. Also it shows us the shitty things that Eva does, it seems like nothing is really being distorted. We can see why she would feel guilty and partially responsible even while also knowing that he was born broken.

I think Lionel was more interested in the ambiguity and mechanics of abuse, the ability of a mother to be just as much at fault as the child, based on what I have heard of the book, but I think Ramsay as a woman was more interested in the idea of actually having given birth to evil and needing to reckon with it as in some way an extension or offshoot of yourself.

Also her artsy movies are actually her earlier ones, funnily enough, while Kevin and You Were Never Really Here pull in bigger stars and get violent. I haven't seen Die My Love yet but my sense is that it is continuing this trajectory. Ratcatcher feels almost like Terrence Malick in comparison.
  - >but I think Ramsay as a woman was more interested in the idea of actually having given birth to evil and needing to reckon with it as in some way an extension or offshoot of yourself.

Not sure you were implying something else, but because it is a traditionally male-coded name, I thought I'd just chime in and say Lionel Shriver is a woman too.
    - Oh yeah that is totally my bad if that is the case, shows my ignorance of the book. Thank you and I guess strike that whole idea!

It does have me thinking, if that is not a factor in play, what accounts for the shift, because I still maintain that the movie does not really strive for much ambiguity in the facts of the matter. I am inclined to attribute it to medium. What we are shown directly by a film carries a weight that is hard to undermine unless you equally show us an alternative, whereas I think the written word has a lot more flexibility in that regard. But I should read the book I guess.
2. I agree it's a masterpiece, but I'm not sure I agree that you need an unreliable narrator device to play around with the ambiguity of Kevin's psychopathy's cause and effect. The movie presents us with a woman who doesn't want to have kids, and struggles to bond with her child. TO her she's struggling because he's psychopathic, but the movie allows the audience to question whether it's the other way around: he's psychopathic because his bond can't honestly bond with her child.

I also disagree that WNTTAK is broadly remembered as part of a subgenre rather than a work of art. Not sure where you're getting that from?
  - >I also disagree that WNTTAK is broadly remembered as part of a subgenre rather than a work of art. Not sure where you're getting that from?

I mean, just saying the title of the movie is shorthand for the subject matter (school shootings), when really the book and movie have the actual atrocity Kevin commits be a secondary element to his relationship w/ his mother. My point is that, looking back, it doesn't 'feel' in any way like the quintennial movie about that subject matter the way it's treated; it doesn't even seem particularly interested in that part of Kevin's life.
3. I think any adaptation of a novel should shoot to include something that is original but adds to the source material. Something that translates uniquely to the cinematic experience. What I appreciated the most about this adaptation was the subplot with her much younger male co-worker. The book is epistolary, only from letters that Eva is writing, so this inclusion is impossible in the original material and I think really helps inform how the world sees Eva outside of herself.  How much joy Eva can expect from the world. 

I love this novel, it only gets more impressive upon rereads. I can hardly fathom how perfectly crafted the generational trauma is to inform all aspects of Eva’s personality, from her fawning over her ridiculous husband, to her carefully constructed veneer of superiority, the groundwork is laid so masterfully. For me, I think the huge telling moment is that if Franklin and Eva had separated when she returned from her trip to Africa, none of the events of the novel and film would have happened, and that’s a real testament to what a hand Eva had in her dynamic with Kevin.
  - I could talk about the book (and Shriver's fiction in general) for hours. I know WNTTAK is a difficult read with a very unlikeable narrator, but by god it's masterful for all the reasons you describe. I remember reading the first few chapters and remarking that the protagonist seemed extremely up her own ass and arrogant; Shriver then spends the rest of the book carefully showing us how unearned and false that confidence is.

## 3. Thoughts on Kevin Smith?

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I'm just old enough to remember the tail end of an era when Kevin Smith still had some auteur cred. When he was considered an interested writer-director with a unique voice.

It's hard to think of filmmaker whose reputation has fallen farther, faster than Smith. In 2025, I think he might be more famous as a potheaded podcaster than as someone who was once grouped with Tarantino, Linklater, etc. as promising young 90s filmmaker.

35+ years into his career, how do we feel about Smith, his body of work, and his place in film history? Is the inspirational story of him selling everything to make *Clerks* on a shoestring budget and then hitting big at Sundance still the salient fact about his career, or is there more there there? 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The problem with Kevin Smith's movies now is that he stopped working collaboratively and with a studio and is  now making smaller indie projects where he does a lot of his own producing and editing and the result is that these movies are very poor and have lots of technical problems and look really bad.

Take a screenshot from Clerks in 1995. The black and white film is iconic. Now look at anything he's done post-2015 and it has almost like a digital sheen of mediocrity on it. Very emblematic of his decline.
2. I still have respect for Kevin Smith. Dogma is one of my favorite movies (yeah, yuk it up), and while there's no denying he fell off a cliff after that, I still respect him a lot. He said on one of his podcasts "People want me to make another Chasing Amy, but I just don't have that kind of movie in my anymore." That's a level of self-awareness and honesty that a lot of people don't have.

Was he ever the world's best filmmaker? Not by a long shot. And we can talk about unrealized potential until the cows come home. He worked best in the low budget sphere, and while Dogma is my favorite, I think having a decently large budget (10 million), a star studded cast and being surrounded in the controversy and fame that followed went to his head and he lost his edge. I think that even with all his ups and downs, Clerks, Mallrats, Chasing Amy and Dogma are still good watches and have their place in the canon. And I'll always have a lot of respect for him.
3. I feel like he's the ideal of what you should strive for if you want to make movies. Making incredibly low budget movies with your friends that get successful enough and get enough powerful people on your side that you can start your own production studio and operate multiple local businesses (with said friends) while still living where you want to live. Maybe his later movies aren't as good as his earlier ones but ultimately i don't think thats the point, and if you're a fan of his style you'd still enjoy them anyway.
  - I really liked *Red State*, wish he had made some more films like it
    - Yeah, I appreciate what he did there and he has a pretty good eye for casting because a lot of those actors broke out after it in other roles. Hell even Yoga Hosers had Austin Butler in it.
4. Smith had something to say in the beginning. He captured a lot of the 90’s and early 2000’s and his films from that time are important cultural milestones. 

That said, he’s made his bag. He no longer HAS to work or try to achieve wide appeal. He can do whatever he wants, and like a lot of us, what he wants to do are the same things he’s always done. 

Thats fine. People who still want to see that can see it, and those who don’t, won’t see it and everyone goes away happy enough. We got a lot of good from Smith. He doesn’t owe us MORE.
5. Oh I love watching Kevin Smith movies so much. Something comforting about the weird way he makes movies. Also just have a lot of nostalgia for those first 5 movies. And how he pulled off Clerks and forced his way into the film world is just a great story. 


His last few movies have been without question some of the worst movies I've ever paid to see in a theater. And plus the tickets are 3 times as expensive to his movies since he does the road show thing. But also I love that he does that. 


And even though these movies are awful and I cringe through the duration of them all, I will continue to see them as long as they get made. 


He reminds me of a time when mainstream culture wasn't so dominated by nerd stuff. I love nerd stuff, don't get me wrong, but there was a certain charm to the days when most people didn't know who Iron Man was.
6. I don't exactly remember the order I saw them in, but somewhere around the year 2000, when I was 12 or 13 and getting into film, Smith's work made a big impact on me. Saw Clerks, Chasing Amy, and Dogma all right around the same time, developed a love for them and the whole narrative of how Smith got started. He was definitely an indie film golden boy. I used to be able to quote most of JaSBSB from start to finish. All of this pushed me to writing and wanting to direct. 

Smith doesn't seem like he's ever really gotten over the fact that he wasn't embraced by Hollywood the same way his contemporaries were. Listening to one of the Dogma commentaries, he gripes about the film not getting enough awards attention. Just a couple years ago, he did a podcast about Licorice Pizza, where he also takes back his earlier negative comments about Magnolia. During this podcast, from 2022, Smith mentions that someone in the Academy told him Chasing Amy was \*this\* close to getting a Screenplay nomination at the Oscars. All these years later, I think Smith is still very miffed that he was never the critical and awards darling that Tarantino, Linklaters, and the Andersons were. Maybe it's why he branched out to other things and does all his filmmaking outside of Hollywood now. 

I don't know where it puts his legacy. I think his horror films are okay. I thought Zack & Miri was okay. I hated Clerks II and have had no desire at all to see Clerks III or the 4:30 Movie. I'll probably never see a new Smith film ever again. It seems like he's more well known now for his An Evening with... DVDs than his films (I've never even seen any of these). At most, I will continue to revist Clerks and Dogma every couple of years. These films still make me laugh.
  - I think the uncomfortable truth as to why he was never as accepted as his 90s contemporaries is simply he's not as good. His films and talent aren't on the same level. I think he knows that as well and resents it to a degree. Kind of like a "fine I'll take my toys and go play over here away from you all".
  - Was he so angry with *Magnolia* primarily because he perceived it as stealing his thunder? Or just that it's an aesthetic polar opposite from the kind of movie Kevin Smith makes?

## 4. How do you categorise a film like Aftersun?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I watched Aftersun last night and was absolutely blown away by how good it was. Rather than discuss it and risk spoiling it for anyone (seriously, go in blind like I did!), I was wondering how film-lovers categorise films like that. I’m not saying that Aftersun is a uniquely uncategorisable film, but how do you usually describe realistic dramas that aren’t genre films (thriller / comedy / romance etc)? 

Just calling it a drama seems a bit vague, and also not very accurate for a film that has most of its actual drama offscreen. I wouldn’t call it arthouse or alternative, as that doesn’t sound right for a straightforward, easily relatable film about a girl and her father on holiday. “Indie film” seems a bit reductive, and makes it sound quirky.

In my mind I call films like this “proper grown-up films”, but that would be unhelpful as a commonly used term, as it implies that genre films are somehow inferior, which they aren’t. It also implies that they’re not suitable for kids, which they are. (Whether kids would actually want to watch them, I don’t know.) 

It could be argued that not everything needs a category, and sometimes categories are actively unhelpful. However at other times it would be helpful to say “You liked Aftersun? Well how about other \_\_\_\_\_\_ films, such as…”.

Hopefully you can see what I’m getting at here! Not looking for objective truths, just interested in how other people define films like this.



### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’d go with character study, which I use pretty loosely. An easy catch-all for any movie where the plot is mundane or doesn’t really have big events, you’re just watching a chunk of a character’s life. If someone could criticize the movie as “nothing happens, it’s just someone being sad for 2 hours”, it’s a character study.
2. It’s inescapable from genre shorthand, but I think it’s a coming of age movie, ultimately. Just not the kind you usually see. 

Even though most of the film is Callum’s POV, the actual framing device of the story is adult Sophie trying to understand a childhood experience that had stayed with her for her whole life. It’s like a retroactive coming of age story, because the processing is happening way later than when the actual plot is taking place.
  - Great point. It certainly is a coming of age story, but if you described it like that to someone, they'd probably have a somewhat distorted impression of what it's like. 

I wonder if "holiday film" works? Holidays in films are almost always transitional events, often with coming of age elements.
3. I see it in the great tradition of the European art film, all of which utilise genre in some ways but often subvert genre by particular use of aesthetics, ambiguity, narrative discontinuity, and utilisation of visibility in filmmaking. Here we have a coming-of-age drama made strange by non-professional performance, use of mediation (in-world filmmaking and watching), tonal presence, colourisation, its own narrative logic, intertextuality (references to Margaret Tait and Clair Denis), queerness, etc.

The point of these films is you can't really do 'if you like X then watch Y' - they aspire to the uniqueness of art. That said, you could watch *All of us Strangers* for a similar vibe.
  - Also for technique, albeit not exactly similar in tone or subject matter, films from Claire Denis/Lynne Ramsay come to mind. Elements of Denis' Chocolat in the framing of childhood memories and, as you mentioned, a direct shot referring to Beau Travail.
    - thank you for that excellent point mice\_in\_my\_anus

## 5. 25 Movies, Many Stars, 0 Hits: Hollywood Falls to New Lows

**Score:** 11
**Body:** "It has been a brutal three months for dramas and comedies. Some were heavily marketed. Many were championed by critics. Most had star power.

But not one of the 25 dramas and comedies that movie companies released in theaters over the past three months has become a hit."

Reasons cited for the underperformance:

* The pandemic strengthened the habit of consuming movies at home.
* During the pandemic, studios started making movies available on digital after as little as 17 days, which diminished the incentive to see movies in theaters.

Streaming is convenient, but it’s really hurt theaters, and I worry that studios are going to make even fewer non-IP movies over time.
